title: A Combinatorial Characterization of Minimax in 0/1 Games
speaker: Shay Moran
abstract: We will discuss a generalization of the celebrated Minimax Theorem (von Neumann, 1928) for binary zero-sum games. A simple game which fails to satisfy Minimax is Ephraim Kishon's “Jewish Poker” (see [1,2] below). In this game, each player picks a number and the larger number wins. The payoff matrix in this game is *infinite triangular*. We show this is the only obstruction: if a game does not contain triangular submatrices of unbounded sizes then the Minimax Theorem holds. This generalizes von Neumann's Minimax Theorem by removing requirements of finiteness or compactness.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from missing minimax hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates the problem by explaining that minimax arguments are common in learning theory but often require finiteness, compactness, or measurability assumptions that are simply assumed away.

proof_stages.label: 0/1 zero-sum games and minimax value
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the two-player 0/1 payoff-matrix setting, mixed strategies as distributions over pure strategies, the finite minimax theorem, and the common value of a game satisfying minimax.

proof_stages.label: Infinite games and the triangular obstruction
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing why classical proofs extend when only one strategy set is infinite or under compactness, the speaker constructs the triangular infinite game called Jewish Poker and computes that its minmax and maxmin values differ.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial characterization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: an arbitrary 0/1 game satisfies minimax if it has no arbitrarily large triangular submatrices, equivalently any failure of minimax is witnessed by arbitrarily large triangular subgames; the later Holzman strengthening replaces arbitrarily large finite triangular subgames by an infinite one.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction strategy reducing to two lemmas
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a counterexample with bounded triangular submatrix size, the proof aims to contradict the bound by deriving an infinite triangular submatrix.

proof_stages.label: Bounded block sequences from the minimax gap
proof_stages.summary: The first lemma constructs finite sets of strategies S_i and T_j of uniformly bounded size whose induced finite games have value below a threshold r for i<j and above r for i>j, using the minmax gap, induction, sampling, and a VC/uniform-law-of-large-numbers bound supplied by the absence of large triangular submatrices.

proof_stages.label: Ramsey extraction of an infinite triangular subgame
proof_stages.summary: The second lemma colors pairs of indices by the two finite payoff matrices between the corresponding blocks, applies infinite Ramsey’s theorem to get a monochromatic subsequence, and then chooses one row and one column position to obtain an infinite Jewish Poker subgame.

proof_stages.label: Holzman’s strengthening via foolable families
proof_stages.summary: In the post-proof discussion the speaker describes a key definition behind Holzman’s stronger result: families of finite subsets of natural numbers that can or cannot be fooled by a divergent positive series, suggesting a combinatorial route to infinite triangular chains.

examples.name: Identity payoff matrix game
examples.note: In response to a question about finite manifestations, the identity matrix game illustrates that bounded triangular patterns need not imply small-support optimal strategies.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to a question, the speaker analyzes the identity payoff matrix, describes the diagonal-one game, identifies the uniform strategy, and states the resulting value as about 1 minus 1/n.

passes.reason: the identity payoff game is a highly regular diagonal game used to refute the suggested small-support consequence

examples.name: Singletons fooled by the harmonic series
examples.note: The family of singleton subsets of the natural numbers is shown to be foolable by the divergent series 1/n, making Holzman’s foolability definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the foolability definition to the family of singletons, chooses the harmonic series, and checks that singleton sums stay bounded while the full series diverges.

passes.reason: the singleton family is an atomic permutation-invariant set system used merely to illustrate foolability by the harmonic series
